_purpose: Micro-Migration Network Map for the LA Acadiana cluster. Documents the specific migration corridors that brought Acadians from Nova Scotia, redistributed populations within Acadiana, and carried people outward to Texas and the Gulf Coast oil fields. The Grand Derangement of 1755 is the founding migration event -- everything after it is a consequence of exile.

migration_corridors.corridor_name: Grand Derangement: Acadian Exile to Louisiana
origin_counties: Nova Scotia (Grand-Pre, Beaubassin, Port Royal, Pisiguit, Cobequid)
France (exile camps in Brittany, Poitou, Normandy)
migration_corridors.destination_area: Attakapas District (St. Martin, Iberia, Lafayette, Vermilion), Opelousas District (St. Landry, Evangeline, Acadia), Lafourche Interior (Assumption)
migration_corridors.scale: Approximately 3,000-4,000 Acadian exiles reached Louisiana between 1765 and 1790, in several waves. They came from multiple points of secondary exile -- some directly from Nova Scotia via the Atlantic coast colonies, some from France after being held in camps for years, some from Saint-Domingue. They constituted the founding population of Acadiana.

evidence.census_evidence: Spanish colonial censuses of the Attakapas and Opelousas districts (1766, 1777, 1785, 1788) list Acadian families by name, origin, and settlement location. These are among the most complete exile-to-settlement records for any forced migration in North American history.
evidence.church_evidence: Catholic sacramental registers at St. Martin de Tours (St. Martinville) and other Acadiana churches document baptisms, marriages, and burials from the 1760s. These registers link Louisiana families to specific Nova Scotian communities of origin.
evidence.surname_clustering: Core Acadian surnames -- Broussard, LeBlanc, Thibodaux, Hebert, Landry, Mouton, Boudreaux, Trahan, Comeaux, Guidry -- dominate the Acadiana population and trace directly to the Grand-Pre, Beaubassin, and other Nova Scotian settlements.

chain_mechanics.description: The Acadian exile was not a single event but a decade-long dispersal. The British expelled the Acadians from Nova Scotia beginning in 1755, scattering them across the Atlantic world -- to the American colonies (Maryland, Pennsylvania, Massachusetts, Connecticut, New York, South Carolina, Georgia), to France, to Saint-Domingue, to England. After years of secondary exile, Acadian families began making their way to Louisiana, drawn by the French-speaking Catholic colonial society under Spanish rule. The Spanish government actively recruited Acadians, providing land grants and supplies. Families that had been separated for years reunited on the bayous of the Attakapas and Opelousas. The migration was kinship-driven -- families sought each other across the Atlantic, and the Louisiana settlement was an act of reassembly as much as colonization.
chain_mechanics.kinship_role: Everything. The Acadian migration to Louisiana was a reconstitution of families and communities shattered by the Grand Derangement. Brothers found brothers. Cousins settled adjacent lots. The long-lot farms along the bayous were arranged by kinship, with extended families clustering on the same waterway.
chain_mechanics.timeline: First arrivals 1765 (directly from Atlantic coast colonies). Major wave 1785 (from France, organized by Henri Peyroux de la Coudreniere). Smaller arrivals through the 1790s.

migration_corridors.narrative_hook: The Grand Derangement scattered {ancestor_name}'s family across the Atlantic -- from the farms of Nova Scotia to the holds of British ships to years of exile in {secondary_exile_location}, and finally, a decade later, to the bayous of Louisiana where the language was the same but nothing else was.
migration_corridors.dignity_note: The Grand Derangement was ethnic cleansing -- a forced removal of an entire people from their homeland by a colonial power. Narrate it as such. The Acadians did not 'leave' Nova Scotia; they were expelled at gunpoint, their farms burned, their families separated, many dying in transit. The Louisiana settlement was an act of survival, not adventure.
migration_corridors.source: Brasseaux, Carl A. The Founding of New Acadia. LSU Press, 1987. Faragher, John Mack. A Great and Noble Scheme: The Tragic Story of the Expulsion of the French Acadians from Their American Homeland. New York: W.W. Norton, 2005.

migration_corridors.corridor_name: Free Creole of Color Settlement in the Opelousas-Attakapas District
migration_corridors.destination_area: St. Landry Parish (Opelousas, Leonville, Frilot Cove), St. Martin Parish, Iberia Parish
migration_corridors.scale: The free Creole of Color community in St. Landry Parish numbered over 1,500 by 1860, making it one of the largest free Black communities in the rural South. Additional communities existed in St. Martin and Iberia parishes.

evidence.census_evidence: Federal and state census records list free people of color separately. The 1850 and 1860 censuses document Creole of Color landowners, slaveholders, and artisans in the Opelousas and Attakapas districts.
evidence.church_evidence: Catholic sacramental registers record baptisms, marriages, and burials of free people of color, often noting racial designation ('f.p.c.', 'f.w.c.'). These records are critical for tracing Creole of Color families.
evidence.surname_clustering: Creole of Color surnames -- Donato, Lemelle, Meullion, Guillory, Fontenot (in Creole context) -- appear in concentrated clusters in St. Landry, St. Martin, and Iberia parishes.

chain_mechanics.description: The free Creole of Color community formed through multiple pathways: manumission by French and Spanish colonial slave owners (sometimes of their own children), migration from New Orleans, immigration from Saint-Domingue after the Haitian Revolution, and natural increase of established free families. The community was French-speaking and Catholic, culturally aligned with the Creole planter class but legally and socially distinct. Creole of Color families established a parallel social world -- their own churches, schools, and social networks -- within the larger slaveholding society. Some achieved remarkable economic independence, owning plantations and enslaved people.
chain_mechanics.kinship_role: Kinship networks within the Creole of Color community were dense and endogamous -- families married within the community to preserve status, property, and cultural identity. The community's cohesion was maintained through Catholic religious practice, French language, and intermarriage.
chain_mechanics.timeline: Colonial period through 1860. The community was established under French and Spanish rule, flourished in the antebellum period, and was legally dismantled during Reconstruction and Jim Crow.

migration_corridors.narrative_hook: The free Creole of Color community around Opelousas was something the rest of the South could not comprehend -- {ancestor_name} was free, French-speaking, Catholic, and Black in a world that insisted those categories could not coexist.
migration_corridors.source: Brasseaux, Carl A., Keith P. Fontenot, and Claude F. Oubre. Creoles of Color in the Bayou Country. University Press of Mississippi, 1994.

migration_corridors.corridor_name: Midwestern Farmers to the Louisiana Rice Prairies
origin_counties: Iowa, Illinois, Kansas, Nebraska -- specifically the Corn Belt and wheat belt counties
migration_corridors.destination_area: Acadia Parish (Crowley, Rayne), Lafayette Parish, Vermilion Parish, western St. Landry Parish
migration_corridors.scale: Several hundred families over two decades. A minority numerically but transformative economically -- they brought the rice cultivation technology that remade the prairie.

evidence.census_evidence: 1900 and 1910 census for Acadia Parish show household heads born in Iowa, Illinois, Kansas. Crowley's early population was heavily Midwestern.
evidence.church_evidence: Protestant (Methodist, Baptist) churches established in Crowley and other rice towns by Midwestern settlers -- unusual in Catholic Acadiana.
evidence.surname_clustering: Anglo and German surnames -- Duson, Abbott, Lyons -- appear in the rice parishes alongside the Acadian surnames.

chain_mechanics.description: The Southern Pacific Railroad promoted land sales along its route through the Louisiana prairies, advertising in Midwestern newspapers. Midwestern farmers recognized the poorly drained prairie as ideal for the irrigated rice cultivation they knew from experience. W.W. Duson (from Iowa) founded Crowley in 1887. The Midwesterners brought machines, English, and Protestant churches into a world that had been French, Catholic, and subsistence-oriented. Their economic success -- rice was profitable -- drew more Midwestern families and displaced the Acadian cattle culture.
chain_mechanics.kinship_role: Less kinship-driven than the Acadian migrations. More driven by railroad land agents and economic opportunity. But once established, Midwestern families recruited neighbors and relatives from their home communities.
chain_mechanics.timeline: First arrivals 1880s; peak 1890-1905; declining after 1910 as available land was taken.

migration_corridors.narrative_hook: The Midwesterners came on the railroad and brought rice, English, and Baptist churches to {ancestor_name}'s French Catholic prairie -- within a generation the open range was gone and the newcomers were the most prosperous farmers in the parish.
migration_corridors.source: Post, Cajun Sketches (1962). Conrad, The Cajuns (1978).

migration_corridors.destination_area: Los Angeles (Watts, Compton), Houston (Fifth Ward, Third Ward), San Francisco/Oakland, Chicago South Side, New Orleans
migration_corridors.scale: Tens of thousands of Black families over five decades. The Black and Creole of Color population in Acadiana stagnated or declined relative to natural increase as outmigration accelerated.

evidence.census_evidence: 1940-1960 census for Los Angeles shows concentrations of Louisiana-born Black residents with Acadiana parish birthplaces. The Watts-Compton corridor of LA had a significant Creole community.
evidence.church_evidence: Catholic parishes in Los Angeles (St. Peter Claver, Corpus Christi) received Creole of Color families from Acadiana who maintained Catholic worship in a Protestant-dominated migration stream.

chain_mechanics.description: The Great Migration from Acadiana followed two primary routes: westward to Houston and Los Angeles (via the Southern Pacific Railroad), and northward to Chicago. The western route was distinctive -- Creole of Color families from St. Landry and surrounding parishes created a significant community in Los Angeles, bringing zydeco music, Catholic practice, and Creole French to Southern California. The push factors were racial violence, economic exclusion, and the collapse of tenant farming. The pull was wartime industrial employment.
chain_mechanics.kinship_role: Church and kinship networks were primary. Catholic Creole families followed Catholic networks to specific parishes in destination cities. Zydeco musicians followed performance circuits.
chain_mechanics.timeline: Trickle 1910-1916; first wave 1917-1920 (WWI); sustained 1920-1940; major wave 1942-1955 (WWII defense industry).

migration_corridors.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} boarded the Southern Pacific heading west in {year}, {pronoun_subject} carried Acadiana to Los Angeles -- the French language, the Catholic faith, and the zydeco that would make Watts sound like Opelousas on Saturday night.
migration_corridors.source: Brasseaux, Fontenot, and Oubre, Creoles of Color (1994). Gregory, James N. The Southern Diaspora. Chapel Hill: UNC Press, 2005.

migration_corridors.corridor_name: Oil Field Migration Into and Out of Acadiana
migration_corridors.destination_area: Lafayette (primary), St. Martin, Iberia, Vermilion, Acadia, St. Landry, Evangeline, Assumption
migration_corridors.scale: Thousands of oil workers and their families over five decades. Lafayette's population doubled and tripled as the oil service industry concentrated there.

evidence.census_evidence: 1920-1960 census for Lafayette shows significant numbers of Texas-, Oklahoma-, and north Louisiana-born household heads. Anglo and Protestant surnames appear alongside the Acadian names.
evidence.church_evidence: Protestant (Baptist, Methodist) churches established in Lafayette and other oil industry towns by non-Cajun oil workers.

chain_mechanics.description: The oil industry brought two migrations: English-speaking outsiders into Acadiana (roughnecks, company managers, engineers) and Cajun men out into the oil fields across the Gulf Coast. Both movements dissolved the cultural boundary that had preserved Cajun French. Cajun men who worked the oil rigs alongside Texans and Oklahomans learned English and brought it home. Anglo oil workers who settled in Lafayette married Cajun women and raised English-speaking children.
chain_mechanics.kinship_role: Less kinship-driven than other Acadiana migrations. Driven by the labor market and company assignments. But Cajun oilfield workers recruited kin into the industry through word of mouth.
chain_mechanics.timeline: Beginning 1901 (Jennings field); accelerating 1920s-1930s; massive during WWII; dominant by 1950s.

migration_corridors.narrative_hook: The oil fields brought Texans into {ancestor_name}'s French-speaking world and sent Cajun men out into an English-speaking one -- and by {year}, the boundary between the two was dissolving in the pay line at the drilling company office.
migration_corridors.source: Bernard, The Cajuns (2003). Post, Cajun Sketches (1962).

internal_movement_patterns.pattern_name: Bayou Margin to Open Prairie (Acadian Expansion)
internal_movement_patterns.description: Acadian families expanded from the initial bayou-margin settlements outward onto the open prairie over two to three generations. The earliest settlers (1760s-1780s) took the bayou frontage; their children and grandchildren moved onto the treeless prairie as the bayou land filled. This movement was incremental -- a few miles per generation -- and tracked by land grant and sale records.
internal_movement_patterns.narrative_hook: {ancestor_name}'s grandparents settled the bayou where the live oaks grew. By {year}, {pronoun_possessive} family was farming the open prairie a mile back from the water, where there was no shade and no neighbor within shouting distance.

internal_movement_patterns.pattern_name: Sugar Workers to Oil Field Workers (Occupational Shift)
internal_movement_patterns.description: Young men from the sugar parishes (St. Martin, Iberia, Assumption) shifted from seasonal sugar cane work to year-round oil field employment as the oil industry expanded after 1901. This was not a geographic migration but an economic one -- men stayed in Acadiana but changed industries, often within the same parish.
internal_movement_patterns.narrative_hook: {ancestor_name} cut cane in October and roughnecked in January -- the oil field paid better than the sugar house, and by {year} the cane knife was hanging on the wall and the hard hat was on the truck seat.

settlement_clusters.description: The densest Acadian settlement in Louisiana, stretching along Bayou Teche from Breaux Bridge through St. Martinville to New Iberia. Long-lot farms, Catholic churches at every bend in the bayou, and the sugar plantations that overlaid the Acadian settlement.

settlement_clusters.cluster_name: Creole of Color communities around Opelousas
settlement_clusters.description: The largest concentration of free Creole of Color families in rural Louisiana, centered on Opelousas, Leonville, and the Frilot Cove area. Catholic, French-speaking, and culturally distinct from both the Acadian and Anglo communities.

Linear settlement along Bayou Lafourche -- long-lot farms arranged in an unbroken chain from Donaldsonville to the Gulf. Every family fronted the bayou because the swamp behind the levee was uninhabitable.

narrative_hooks: The Grand Derangement was the founding event -- {ancestor_name}'s family was expelled from Nova Scotia, scattered across the Atlantic, and reassembled in Louisiana a decade later. Everything about Acadiana starts with that exile.
The free Creole of Color community around Opelousas was {ancestor_name}'s world -- free, French, Catholic, and Black in a South that could not comprehend the combination.
The Midwesterners brought rice, English, and fences to {ancestor_name}'s open prairie -- and within a generation, the vacherie was memory and the rice paddies were reality.
The Great Migration carried {ancestor_name}'s family from the Opelousas dance halls to the zydeco clubs of Watts -- the same music, the same faith, three thousand miles from home.

The oil industry dissolved the boundary that had preserved {ancestor_name}'s French -- when the Texans arrived and the Cajuns went to work on the rigs, the two languages mixed on the drill floor and English won.
source_references: Brasseaux, Carl A. The Founding of New Acadia. Baton Rouge: Louisiana State University Press, 1987.
Faragher, John Mack. A Great and Noble Scheme. New York: W.W. Norton, 2005.
Brasseaux, Carl A., Keith P. Fontenot, and Claude F. Oubre. Creoles of Color in the Bayou Country. Jackson: University Press of Mississippi, 1994.
Post, Lauren C. Cajun Sketches. Baton Rouge: Louisiana State University Press, 1962.
Bernard, Shane K. The Cajuns. Jackson: University Press of Mississippi, 2003.
Conrad, Glenn R., ed. The Cajuns. Lafayette: Center for Louisiana Studies, 1978.
Gregory, James N. The Southern Diaspora. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2005.
